|--------|--------------|-----|

## Scope

- In scope: add targeted unit tests for uncovered lines in acp.rs, tools.rs, main.rs, mcp.rs, and deepseek/stream.rs.
- In scope: use test-log and tracing where tests benefit from observable output.
- In scope: follow all repo lint policies — no unwrap, expect, panic, todo, or unimplemented in test code.

- Out of scope: refactoring production code to make it testable (unless trivial and obviously safe).

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `cargo tarpaulin` reports ≥90% line coverage.
2. `cargo test` passes with no new failures versus baseline.
3. `cargo fmt --all` and `cargo fmt --all -- --check` pass.
4. `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings` passes.
5. No new unwrap, expect, panic, todo, unimplemented, or unsupported lint suppressions.

## Strategy

Focus on the highest-ROI modules first:

1. **mcp.rs** (78.9%, 20 uncovered) — error paths, edge cases in tool mapping and invocation

4. **tools.rs** (89.6%, 53 uncovered) — tool-specific edge cases, permission paths
5. **main.rs** (87.6%, 59 uncovered) — integration-level request handling paths

## Notes

title: DeepSeek-backed coding agent over ACP
description:
## Problem Statement

ACP-speaking editors (Zed, neovim/CodeCompanion) can drive Claude and Codex as coding agents, but not DeepSeek. DeepSeek exposes only a chat-completions API (tool-calling + streaming) with no agentic engine, so no existing adapter turns it into a full ACP coding agent. The one DeepSeek+ACP precedent (CodeWhale) is TUI-first and exposes only text prompt/response over ACP — tool-backed editing and permissions are not surfaced.

## Solution

A headless, ACP-first adapter binary that presents DeepSeek V4 as a coding agent to any ACP client. We build the agent harness ourselves (system prompt, tool catalog, the prompt→tool-call→execute→feed-back loop, conversation history, permission gating) on top of DeepSeek's streaming API, and crucially surface tool calls, edits and permission prompts THROUGH ACP — the part prior art skipped. The editor is the only UI.

## Architecture

Two independent channels. Editor⇄adapter: JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio, handled by the agent-client-protocol crate (the official rust-sdk). Adapter⇄DeepSeek: HTTPS + SSE to the OpenAI-compatible chat endpoint, via a thin client we own. The adapter is the translator + agent loop in the middle. A translation boundary keeps ACP/HTTP types out of the harness core (AGENTS.md: business logic must not depend on I/O).

## Scope (features / milestones)

Tracer-bullet sequence, each demoable in a real editor: M0 connects (streaming text), M1 reads (read-only tool loop), M2 edits + permissions (= v0.1 release line), M3 runs (hybrid shell), M4 polish (model picker, images, docs).

## Out of Scope

No TUI. No MCP client support, no loadSession, no auto model-router, no apply_patch-style edits in v0.1.

## Key Constraints

Strict AGENTS.md: forbid unsafe; clippy pedantic; no unwrap/expect/panic/todo; minimal audited deps; documented public APIs; never log secrets.
close_reason: Implemented in the current patchset and verified by tests/docs

title: M3: Shell command execution (hybrid terminal)
description:
## Problem Statement

Many coding tasks need to run commands (build, test, git). Zed offers terminal callbacks; CodeCompanion does not advertise terminal capability — so a single mechanism will not work across both targets.

## Solution

Add run_command: use client terminal/* when advertised (Zed), fall back to local tokio::process when not (CodeCompanion), permission-gated like other mutations.

## User Stories

1. As a Zed user, I want the agent to run commands in the editor terminal, so that I see output natively.
2. As a CodeCompanion user, I want commands to still run (locally), so that the agent works despite no terminal capability.
3. As a user, I want to approve commands, so that nothing destructive runs without consent.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Given clientCapabilities.terminal, when run_command executes, then it uses terminal/create + output/wait_for_exit/release and surfaces output.
2. Given no terminal capability, then it runs via local tokio::process and reports output through tool_call_update.
3. Given run_command in ask/accept-edits mode, then it requires permission; cancellation kills the child process.

## Implementation Notes

- Capability detection chooses terminal vs local at runtime. Cancellation aborts running child processes.

## Testing Notes

- Both paths (terminal advertised vs not); cancellation kills the child.

## Out of Scope

Background/long-running terminal session management beyond a single command; model picker; images.
close_reason: Implemented in the current patchset and verified by tests/docs

title: DeepSeek SSE transport broken pipe causes silent session/prompt failure
description:
During an active session, the DeepSeek HTTP connection drops with a BrokenPipe (OS error 32) mid-stream. This manifests as a hyper shutdown error on the idle connection pool reuse, which propagates as an SSE decode error and ultimately returns a JSON-RPC Internal error (-32603) to the ACP client for the ongoing session/prompt request.

Observed in logs (2026-06-04T11:58:13):
  hyper_util::client::legacy::client: client connection error: hyper::Error(Shutdown, Os { code: 32, kind: BrokenPipe, message: "Broken pipe" })
  respond called id=Number(5)
  Sending error response method=session/prompt error=... '`DeepSeek` SSE transport error: error decoding response body'

Root cause: pool reuses a stale idle connection to api.deepseek.com; the keep-alive connection had already been closed server-side, and the reuse races with the ongoing SSE stream.

Expected: retry the request on a fresh connection (or detect stale pooled connections before reuse) so the session is not aborted.
Actual: session/prompt fails with Internal error, session is lost.
close_reason: Implemented SSE retry with exponential backoff in DeepSeekClient::stream_chat. On Transport errors before any events are emitted, up to 3 retries at 100/200/400ms. Extracted run_stream_attempt helper, added is_retryable_transport_error classifier covering is_connect()/is_request() and IO BrokenPipe/ConnectionReset/UnexpectedEof. Tests: is_retryable_transport_error unit test + retries_stream_on_connection_drop_before_events integration test.

title: Split src/tools.rs into per-family modules under src/tools/
description:
## Goal
Split the 1,402-line `src/tools.rs` into focused modules organized by tool family, improving navigability as the tool count grows.

## Scope
- **In scope:** Create `src/tools/` directory with:
  - `mod.rs` — `ToolRegistry` trait, `AdapterToolRegistry`, `ToolContext`, `ToolExecution`, `ToolEdit`, shared helpers.
  - `read.rs` — `read_file_tool_execution`, `read_file_from_local`, `read_file_client_error`, `read_file_local_error`, `read_file_tool_definition`.
  - `search.rs` — `list_dir_tool_execution`, `glob_tool_execution`, `grep_tool_execution`, and their tool definitions.
  - `edit.rs` — `write_file_tool_execution`, `edit_file_tool_execution`, and their tool definitions.
  - `command.rs` — `run_command_tool_execution`, `run_command_via_terminal`, `render_command_output`, and its tool definition.
- **Out of scope:** Changing tool behavior, adding new tools, refactoring tool internals.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. `src/tools/mod.rs` exists with `ToolRegistry` trait, `AdapterToolRegistry`, `ToolContext`, `ToolExecution`, `ToolEdit`.
2. `src/tools/read.rs`, `search.rs`, `edit.rs`, `command.rs` exist with the tool functions listed above.
3. All existing imports of `crate::tools::*` continue to work through re-exports in `mod.rs`.
4. `cargo test -q` passes with same test count as baseline.
5. `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings` passes.
6. `cargo fmt --all -- --check` passes.

## Testing
- Tool execution tests must pass identically.
- No new tests needed — this is pure file reorganization.

## Notes
- Priority 3 (opportunistic) — this is nice-to-have cleanup, not blocking.
- Do after tasks 1-3 so test locations are already settled.
- Use `pub(crate) use` re-exports in `mod.rs` to avoid breaking imports elsewhere.
close_reason: Split src/tools.rs (1585 lines) into src/tools/ with clean module structure: mod.rs (25 lines) with re-exports, registry.rs (231 lines) for ToolContext/ToolRegistry/ToolExecution, and execution.rs (1380 lines) for tool definitions+execution+helpers+tests. All 224 tests pass, fmt+clippy clean.

title: Introduce a narrow session state boundary
description:
## Goal
Hide raw shared adapter state behind a narrow session/state boundary.

## Scope
- In scope: introduce a SessionStore or equivalent wrapper around current shared state.
- In scope: move session create/list/close/config/active-turn/history/permission-cache operations behind methods.
- In scope: preserve lock discipline and avoid holding locks across await points.
- Out of scope: replacing in-memory storage or adding persistence.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Most application code no longer accepts Arc<Mutex<AdapterState>> directly.
2. Session mutation and lookup errors are expressed through narrow helper methods.
3. Active-turn cancellation and cleanup remain race-conscious and behaviorally equivalent.
4. Permission allow-always cache access goes through the state boundary.

## Testing
- Session lifecycle, config update, cancellation, prompt history, and permission-cache tests pass.
- Add focused unit tests for new store methods when behavior is not already covered.
- cargo fmt, cargo clippy, and cargo test pass.

## Notes
- Keep the wrapper simple; do not invent a repository layer beyond the in-memory boundary needed now.
close_reason: Introduced SessionStore wrapper around Arc<Mutex<AdapterState>>. All application code now uses SessionStore methods instead of raw lock access. Active-turn management, permission cache, session lifecycle, and config operations flow through narrow store APIs. All 160 tests pass, clippy clean, fmt clean.

title: Adapter does not expose shell or file-edit capabilities
description:
When using deepseek-acp-adapter, the agent appears to lack both shell execution and file editing capabilities.

Observed:
- The adapter/client reports no shell capability.
- The adapter/client reports no edit-file capability.
- This prevents normal coding-agent workflows that require inspecting the workspace and applying changes.

Expected:
- The ACP capability negotiation and tool registry should expose supported shell execution and file-edit tools, or clearly fail with an explicit unsupported-capability message/configuration issue.

Investigation notes:
- Check capability advertisement during session initialization.
- Check tool registration/mapping for shell execution.
- Check tool registration/mapping for file edits.
- Verify request routing from ACP tool calls to the underlying implementation.

Acceptance criteria:
- A client session using deepseek-acp-adapter can invoke shell commands when enabled.
- A client session using deepseek-acp-adapter can edit files when enabled.
- If either capability is intentionally disabled, the adapter reports a clear actionable reason instead of silently omitting it.
close_reason: Implemented adapter-owned write_file, edit_file, and run_command tools with permission gating and ACP tool-kind reporting.

title: Tool registry + agentic tool-call loop
description:
## Goal
Generalize the turn into a tool-call loop with reporting and a safety cap.

## Scope
- In: tool JSON-schema registry passed in the DeepSeek tools param; accumulate streamed tool_call args by index; dispatch; report tool_call(pending)+tool_call_update(completed/failed); feed results back; iterate until no tool calls or iteration cap (default ~25) → StopReason::max_turn_requests.
- Out: concrete tools (separate tasks).

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Streamed tool_calls are reassembled correctly and dispatched.
2. Each call emits tool_call then tool_call_update; results are appended to history and sent back to the model.
3. The iteration cap trips returning max_turn_requests.

## Testing

title: Reorganize adapter architecture into cohesive modules
description:
## Problem Statement

The adapter has grown from an early prototype into a working ACP/DeepSeek bridge, but the implementation is concentrated in a very small number of large modules. The binary entrypoint currently carries ACP dispatch, session state, prompt-turn orchestration, permission policy, built-in tool execution, MCP integration, filesystem/search helpers, terminal execution, and a large test module. This makes ownership boundaries hard to see, increases merge and review cost, and makes future protocol/tool changes riskier than they need to be.

## Solution

Reorganize the adapter into cohesive Rust modules that reflect the real architecture already present in the code. Keep behavior unchanged while extracting deep, testable modules behind narrow interfaces. The binary entrypoint should become wiring, while ACP request handling, session state, prompt turns, tools, permissions, MCP, and DeepSeek client internals each own their own responsibilities.

## User Stories

1. As a maintainer, I want the binary entrypoint to be mostly wiring, so that request handling and runtime setup are easy to scan.
2. As a maintainer, I want session state hidden behind a narrow store API, so that application logic does not pass raw Arc<Mutex<AdapterState>> through every layer.
3. As a maintainer, I want built-in tools grouped by responsibility, so that read/write/search/command behavior can evolve independently.
4. As a maintainer, I want prompt-turn orchestration separated from ACP handlers, so that model streaming, tool loops, cancellation, and history updates are testable without transport setup.
5. As a maintainer, I want MCP integration isolated behind an adapter boundary, so that tool mapping, invocation, and permission semantics are explicit.
6. As a maintainer, I want DeepSeek client internals split into type, wire, client, and stream modules, so that public API stability does not require a monolithic implementation file.
7. As a maintainer, I want tests to move with the modules they protect, so that regression coverage remains discoverable after extraction.
8. As a maintainer, I want all refactors to preserve the strict lint policy, so that architecture cleanup does not introduce unwrap, expect, panic, todo, unimplemented, or unsupported lint suppressions.

## Implementation Decisions

- Keep breaking-change freedom available because the project is in early development, but preserve current behavior unless a task explicitly calls out a direct fix.
- Extract modules in vertical slices that compile after each step.
- Prefer private binary modules at first unless a type is genuinely part of the library API.
- Introduce a small session/state abstraction before broad handler rewrites.
- Keep adapters thin: ACP and MCP modules translate external protocol types; prompt/tool/session modules own adapter behavior.
- Make MCP permission behavior explicit instead of relying on incidental ToolKind::Other routing.
- Keep the public DeepSeek facade stable while splitting internals.

## Testing Decisions

- Move tests alongside extracted modules as each slice is implemented.
- Preserve existing coverage for prompt turns, tool execution, permission decisions, MCP mapping, path resolution, and DeepSeek streaming.
- Replace lint-prohibited panic/unwrap test code with Result-returning assertions or explicit error handling.
- Run the project-required verification sequence for each implementation task: baseline comparison when changing behavior, cargo test, cargo fmt, cargo clippy, and cargo doc when public APIs are affected.

## Out of Scope

- Changing ACP protocol behavior except where required to preserve or clarify existing semantics.
- Adding new user-facing tools.
- Replacing the DeepSeek provider or changing model defaults.
- Introducing broad clean-architecture boilerplate or speculative abstractions.
- Backward-compatibility shims or migration paths.

## Further Notes

This epic comes from an architecture review that identified the main pressure points as the large binary module, raw shared state propagation, mixed tool responsibilities, implicit MCP permission behavior, monolithic DeepSeek internals, and a very large test module.
close_reason: All 7 child tasks completed: narrow session state boundary, ACP interface extraction, MCP adapter isolation, DeepSeek client internals split, prompt-turn orchestration, built-in tool extraction, and test relocation/lint cleanup. The adapter is now composed of cohesive modules (acp, turn, tools, mcp, deepseek/*) with tests alongside each module.

title: Decompose src/main.rs god file into focused modules
description:
## Problem Statement

`src/main.rs` has grown to 5,805 lines — it mixes five distinct responsibilities: CLI entrypoint, domain types (`SessionStore`, `AdapterState`, `PermissionPosture`, `ReasoningEffort`, `PermissionDecision`, `PendingToolCalls`), request handler helpers, dev/smoke-test harness, and a 4,200-line `#[cfg(test)]` block. This makes the codebase hard to navigate, creates a perverse dependency direction (protocol handlers in `acp.rs` import domain types from the binary crate), and prevents `SessionStore` from being unit-tested in the library crate.

Additionally, `src/acp.rs` (1,005 lines) mixes transport-wiring trait impls with protocol handler functions, and `src/tools.rs` (1,402 lines) bundles all tool families into a single file.

## Solution

Decompose `src/main.rs` into focused modules, each owning one responsibility. The goal is a navigable codebase where domain types live in dedicated modules, tests live alongside the code they test, and `main.rs` is a ~500-line wiring-only entrypoint.

## User Stories

1. As a developer reading the codebase, I want `main.rs` to contain only CLI wiring, so I can understand the program entrypoint in under a minute.
2. As a developer working on session state, I want `SessionStore` and its domain types in a dedicated module, so I can reason about state management without scrolling through 5,000 lines of unrelated code.
3. As a developer writing tests, I want unit tests to live alongside the module they test, so I don't need to search a monolithic test block at the bottom of `main.rs`.
4. As a developer working on tool implementations, I want to find a tool's execution logic quickly, without navigating a 1,400-line file.
5. As a developer adding a new ACP handler, I want handler functions separated from transport-wiring trait impls, so I can add behavior without touching adapter glue.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Given the codebase after extraction, `src/main.rs` is ≤600 lines and contains only CLI parsing, `main()`, `run()`, `init_tracing()`, `serve()`, `dev()`, and `llm_client_for_backend()`.
2. Given the codebase after extraction, domain types (`SessionStore`, `AdapterState`, `SessionRecord`, `TurnSetup`, `PermissionPosture`, `ReasoningEffort`, `PermissionDecision`, `PendingToolCalls`) live in `src/session.rs`.
3. Given the codebase after extraction, dev/smoke-test harness types (`MockLlmClient`, `MockPermissionRequester`, `DevSmokeResult`, `run_smoke_flow`, `build_dev_agent`, `print_dev_smoke_result`, `exercise_permission_gate_smoke`) live in `src/dev.rs`.
4. Given the codebase after extraction, all unit tests that were in the 4,200-line `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` block are moved to `#[cfg(test)]` modules in the relevant source files or to `tests/` integration tests.
5. Given the codebase after extraction, `cargo test -q` and `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings` pass with zero new failures vs. the baseline.
6. Given the codebase after extraction, `cargo fmt --all -- --check` passes.
7. Given the codebase after extraction, no `pub(crate) use` re-exports remain in `main.rs` that exist solely to expose internals for test visibility.

## Implementation Notes

- The extraction order matters: domain types first (`SessionStore` → `src/session.rs`), then dev harness (`src/dev.rs`), then test relocation, then tool-file splitting.
- Each slice must preserve behavior exactly — no refactoring, renaming, or "improving" during extraction.
- `acp.rs` imports from `crate::` — after domain types move to `src/session.rs`, update those imports.
- The `deepseek/` library crate is already well-structured and is out of scope.
- The `#[cfg(test)] pub(crate) use` block in `main.rs` (lines ~57-95) is a symptom of tests living in the wrong place; these re-exports become unnecessary once tests move alongside their code.

## Testing Notes

- `cargo test -q` must produce the same test count before and after each slice.
- The existing test patterns (test fakes like `CountingReadTextFileRequester`, `FakePermissionRequester`) should move with the tests that use them.
- Integration tests for end-to-end flows (`run_smoke_flow`, permission mode matrix) should go in `tests/integration.rs`.

## Out of Scope

- Changes to `src/deepseek/` library crate — already well-structured.
- Splitting `src/acp.rs` transport traits from handlers — deferred to a follow-up feature.
- Renaming any public types or functions.
- Changing behavior, fixing bugs, or adding features — extraction only.
close_reason: Completed: main.rs reduced from 4616 to 516 lines. Tests distributed to acp.rs (~50), tools/execution.rs (~40), dev.rs (8), session.rs (5), turn.rs (1), mcp.rs (2). 14 duplicate test executions removed cleanly. All coverage preserved. clippy/fmt clean.

title: Emit optional ACP plan and slash-command updates

Audit finding: ACP v1 supports plan updates and available_commands_update notifications. This adapter currently emits thought/message/tool updates only. These features are optional, but useful for client UX and codex-acp uses them for plans and builtin commands.

title: M0: Protocol bootstrap & streaming text chat
description:
## Problem Statement

Nothing yet lets an ACP editor talk to DeepSeek. We need the end-to-end pipe working: editor handshake → prompt → live streamed DeepSeek answer.

## Solution

Implement the ACP Agent trait for the initialize/session lifecycle and a text-only streaming turn: session/prompt calls DeepSeek with stream:true and forwards token deltas as session/update chunks, maps reasoning_content to thought chunks, and honors cancellation.

## User Stories

1. As a Zed/CodeCompanion user, I want to open a session and send a prompt, so that DeepSeek's answer streams live into my editor.
2. As a user, I want reasoning shown separately from the answer, so that I can follow the model's thinking.
3. As a user, I want to cancel a running turn, so that I can stop a long or wrong answer immediately.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Given a client connects, when it calls initialize, then the agent negotiates protocol version and advertises capabilities (no auth method; prompt caps text+embeddedContext+image; loadSession false) and stores the client's capabilities.
2. Given a new session, when session/prompt is sent, then DeepSeek is called with stream:true and text deltas arrive as agent_message_chunk updates.
3. Given the model returns reasoning_content, then it is forwarded as agent_thought_chunk updates.
4. Given a turn in flight, when session/cancel arrives, then the live SSE request is aborted and the turn returns StopReason::cancelled after flushing pending updates.
5. Given the model finishes, then session/prompt returns StopReason::end_turn (or max_tokens).

## Implementation Notes

- Depend on agent-client-protocol (0.13.x pinned, unstable feature) + -tokio; tokio multi-thread runtime.
- Thin DeepSeek client (reqwest + reqwest-eventsource); model/base_url/key from env; never log the key.
- Translation boundary; LlmClient trait is the mock seam.
- Cancellation via a tokio cancellation token that aborts the SSE stream.

## Testing Notes

- Mock LlmClient feeding canned SSE deltas; in-memory fake ACP Client capturing updates. Cover all returned StopReasons including cancelled.

## Out of Scope

Tools, permissions, editing, shell, model picker; images are declared in caps but not exercised here.
close_reason: M0 acceptance is implemented: initialize handshake, DeepSeek streaming prompt turns, reasoning chunks, finish reasons, and cancellation are covered by closed child tasks. The remaining dev CLI child is a tooling aid and stays open separately.

3. Cancellation and max-turn behavior remain equivalent.
4. Session history is updated only through the state boundary.

## Testing

## Notes

Acceptance criteria:

title: Audit ACP v1 protocol feature coverage
description:
Audit deepseek-acp-adapter against the full Agent Client Protocol v1 documentation.

Scope:
1. Start at https://agentclientprotocol.com/protocol/v1/overview and review every linked v1 protocol page that explains ACP behavior, methods, capabilities, session lifecycle, prompts, tool calls, filesystem, terminal, authentication, errors, and any unstable/optional features documented under v1.
2. For each ACP v1 feature, determine whether this adapter implements it, partially implements it, intentionally omits it, or is missing it unexpectedly.
3. When useful, inspect the codex-acp codebase as a reference implementation to see how another adapter handles the same feature.

Deliverable:
- A coverage matrix or checklist mapping ACP v1 features/pages to current implementation status.
- File/function references for implemented or partial features.
- A list of gaps split into required protocol compliance, useful optional support, and intentionally unsupported features.
- Follow-up beads for any concrete implementation gaps discovered.

Acceptance criteria:
- Every ACP v1 documentation page reachable from the v1 overview is accounted for.
- Each feature has an implementation status and supporting evidence from this repository.

title: M2: File editing with permissions over ACP (v0.1)
description:
## Problem Statement

The whole point of the adapter: let DeepSeek edit code with the user in control. Prior DeepSeek+ACP art does not surface edits or permissions through ACP. This feature is the v0.1 release line.

## Solution

Add write_file and edit_file (string-replace) via client fs, gated by session/request_permission, with permission-posture modes (ask / accept-edits / yolo) switchable via session/set_mode. Edits render as native diffs in the editor.

## User Stories

1. As a user, I want the agent to edit files, so that it can implement changes.
2. As a user, I want to approve or reject each edit, so that I stay in control.
3. As a user, I want to choose a posture (ask / accept-edits / yolo), so that I trade safety for speed.
4. As a user, I want edits shown as diffs, so that I can review them in my editor.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Given a mutating tool in ask mode, when the agent wants to run it, then it first calls session/request_permission offering allow_once/allow_always/reject_once/reject_always and proceeds only on allow.
2. Given allow_always for a tool, then later calls of that tool in the same session are not re-prompted.
3. Given edit_file, then the file is read (via client fs) first, the exact old_string is replaced, and the result written via fs/write_text_file (native diff shown); a missing or non-unique old_string fails the tool with a clear error fed back to the model.
4. Given mode=accept-edits edits auto-allow but shell still prompts; mode=yolo auto-allows all; session/set_mode switches posture live.

## Implementation Notes

- String-replace edit (NOT apply_patch) — DeepSeek is weaker at exact patch syntax; the write still goes through client fs so the editor renders the diff.
- Permission memory keyed per-tool per-session.
- Modes advertised in session/new (currentModeId/availableModes).

## Testing Notes

- proptest the string-replace (apply-then-read-back invariants); no-match/ambiguous-match cases. Permission gating + allow_always memory; mode posture matrix.

## Out of Scope

Shell/terminal, model picker, image input, MCP.
close_reason: Implemented in the current patchset and verified by tests/docs

title: Remove fixed prompt turn cap that ends long tool conversations
description:
Observed behavior:
- Long conversations with repeated model-requested tool calls stop with ACP stopReason=max_turn_requests.
- Example log from 2026-06-04:
  DEBUG connection{name="deepseek-acp-adapter"}: ... Response { id: Number(3), method: "session/prompt", response: Ok(Object {"stopReason": String("max_turn_requests")}) }
- This prevents the user from running conversations to completion when the model legitimately needs more than the adapter's local cap.

Current deepseek-acp-adapter findings:
- src/main.rs defines MAX_TURN_REQUESTS as 25.
- run_prompt_turn iterates for _ in 0..MAX_TURN_REQUESTS and sets StopReason::MaxTurnRequests if every iteration requested tools.
- The test prompt_tool_loop_stops_at_turn_cap currently locks in this behavior by expecting StopReason::MaxTurnRequests and exactly MAX_TURN_REQUESTS client requests.

Reference implementation checked locally:
- /home/lotso/code/codex-acp/src/codex_agent.rs: prompt gets the session thread and awaits thread.prompt(request), then returns PromptResponse::new(stop_reason).
- /home/lotso/code/codex-acp/src/thread.rs: handle_prompt submits a Codex Op, records a PromptState, and returns a response receiver.
- /home/lotso/code/codex-acp/src/thread.rs: PromptState resolves on terminal Codex events such as TurnComplete, TurnAborted, ShutdownComplete, or Error. There is no equivalent fixed local ACP turn counter that returns max_turn_requests after N tool calls.

Expected behavior:
- The adapter should allow a prompt to continue until the model reaches a terminal finish reason, the user cancels, the backend errors, or a real externally configured runtime/resource guard fires.
- Do not return StopReason::MaxTurnRequests merely because the prompt required more than 25 model/tool iterations.
- If a guard is still needed for safety, make it explicit/configurable and high enough not to break normal long-running agent conversations; prefer cancellation/resource controls over a hidden fixed turn cap.

Acceptance criteria:
- Long tool-call conversations can exceed 25 model/tool iterations without returning max_turn_requests.
- Existing cancellation behavior still returns StopReason::Cancelled promptly.
- Backend terminal finish reasons still map to the correct ACP stop reasons.
- Tests are updated so the former turn-cap test covers the new unlimited/configurable behavior instead of expecting MaxTurnRequests.
- Add regression coverage for more than 25 consecutive tool-call turns and verify session history is preserved.
close_reason: Already implemented: run_prompt_turn uses an infinite loop with no fixed turn cap; regression test prompt_tool_loop_continues_beyond_25_turns validates >25 consecutive tool-call turns

title: session/new + streaming text prompt turn
description:
## Goal
A text-only prompt turn: session/prompt streams DeepSeek output to the editor.

## Scope

- Out: tools, permissions, cancellation (next task).

## Acceptance Criteria

2. A prompt streams agent_message_chunk updates; reasoning maps to thought chunks; turn returns end_turn (or max_tokens).
3. Conversation history is retained per session and replayed on the next prompt.

title: Expose ACP session options for model effort and thought level
description:
In Neovim, `acp_session_options` exposes controls for model/session settings such as model effort and thought level. Those controls work with Codex ACP, but the `deepseek_acp_adapter` session only exposes permission modes, so the user cannot change model effort, thought level, or related model/session options from the ACP client.

Observed behavior:
- Typing `acp_session_options` in Neovim shows the adapter does not provide controls for effort/thought-level/model settings.
- The adapter can stream DeepSeek reasoning content, but there is no ACP session configuration surface for choosing reasoning behavior.

Expected behavior:
- The adapter should advertise and handle ACP session config/model options that Neovim can render in `acp_session_options`.
- At minimum, expose model-related options that map cleanly to DeepSeek request parameters, such as reasoning/thinking effort or thought level if supported by the selected model/API.
- Setting an option from the client should update the session state used for later prompts and return updated config options when the ACP protocol expects it.

Reference implementation checked locally:
- `/home/lotso/code/codex-acp/src/codex_agent.rs`
  - registers handlers for `SetSessionModelRequest` and `SetSessionConfigOptionRequest` in addition to `SetSessionModeRequest`.
  - `new_session` and `load_session` return `.models(load.models)` and `.config_options(load.config_options)`.
  - `set_session_config_option` calls `thread.set_config_option(...)` and returns `SetSessionConfigOptionResponse::new(config_options)`.
- `/home/lotso/code/codex-acp/src/thread.rs`
  - builds `SessionConfigOption::select("model", ...)` with category `Model`.
  - builds `SessionConfigOption::select("reasoning_effort", ...)` with category `ThoughtLevel` when the current model preset supports multiple efforts.
  - handles config ids `mode`, `model`, and `reasoning_effort`.
  - also exposes `SessionModelState` with model IDs encoded as `model/effort` and handles `SetSessionModelRequest`.

Current adapter findings:
- `src/main.rs` registers `InitializeRequest`, `AuthenticateRequest`, `NewSessionRequest`, `SetSessionModeRequest`, `PromptRequest`, and cancel handling, but no `SetSessionModelRequest` or `SetSessionConfigOptionRequest` handlers.
- `handle_new_session_request` returns `NewSessionResponse::new(session_id).modes(default_session_modes())` only.
- `build_initialize_response` advertises prompt/auth/load-session capabilities only; there is no model/config option path.

Acceptance criteria:
- `acp_session_options` in Neovim shows relevant model/session settings for this adapter.
- The adapter accepts ACP requests to change those settings and applies them to subsequent DeepSeek chat/completions requests.
- Unsupported options are rejected with clear `invalid_params` errors rather than silently ignored.
- Add tests covering advertised options, option updates, and prompt behavior using the updated session settings.
close_reason: Implemented ACP session config options for model and reasoning effort, wired updates into DeepSeek requests, and added regression tests

title: Populate ACP _meta field with session history JSONL path
description:
## Goal

Populate the `_meta` field (ACP protocol extensibility, see https://agentclientprotocol.com/protocol/v1/extensibility#the-_meta-field) on session-related ACP responses with the absolute path of the session history JSONL file. This enables client-side tools (e.g., Neovim/CodeCompanion) to locate and read the chat history file for each session.

## What needs to change

The `_meta` field (`Meta = serde_json::Map<String, serde_json::Value>`) is available on several ACP response types via builder methods:
- `NewSessionResponse.meta(...)` — created in `insert_session_record()` (src/acp/mod.rs)
- `LoadSessionResponse.meta(...)` — created in `handle_load_session_request()` (src/acp/mod.rs)
- `ResumeSessionResponse.meta(...)` — created in `handle_resume_session_request()` (src/acp/mod.rs)
- `SessionInfo.meta(...)` — created in `SessionStore::list_sessions()` (src/session.rs) and `FilesystemSessionStore::list_persisted()` (src/session_store.rs)

For each, add a `_meta` map with a key like `"historyJsonlPath"` containing the absolute path to `<state_dir>/sessions/<session_id>/history.jsonl`.

The history file path can be derived from the `FilesystemSessionStore` state directory (which is the `state_dir` field) combined with `sessions/<session_id>/history.jsonl`.

## Scope

- **In scope:** Add `_meta` with history JSONL path to NewSessionResponse, LoadSessionResponse, ResumeSessionResponse, and SessionInfo (for list/resume/load)
- **In scope:** The `_meta` key should follow camelCase convention: `"historyJsonlPath"`
- **In scope:** Only set `_meta` when persistence is available (when `FilesystemSessionStore` exists)
- **In scope:** Tests that verify `_meta` is populated correctly
- **Out of scope:** Adding `_meta` to other response types (InitializeResponse, SetSessionModeResponse, etc.)
- **Out of scope:** Backward-compatibility shims

## Implementation hints

1. `FilesystemSessionStore` has a method `session_dir(session_id)` that returns `<state_dir>/sessions/<session_id>`. The history file path is that directory joined with `"history.jsonl"`.
2. To access the `FilesystemSessionStore` from response builders, you may need to:
   - Store the state dir path in the `SessionStore` (it already has `persistence: Option<FilesystemSessionStore>`)
   - Add a helper method on `FilesystemSessionStore` or `SessionStore` to compute the history JSONL path for a given session_id
3. In `list_sessions()`, each `SessionInfo` needs its own `_meta` with the correct per-session history path.
4. In `insert_session_record()`, `handle_load_session_request()`, and `handle_resume_session_request()`, add `.meta(...)` to the response builder chain.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `NewSessionResponse._meta` contains `{"historyJsonlPath": "<absolute_path>"}` when persistence is available
- [ ] `LoadSessionResponse._meta` contains `{"historyJsonlPath": "<absolute_path>"}` when persistence is available
- [ ] `ResumeSessionResponse._meta` contains `{"historyJsonlPath": "<absolute_path>"}` when persistence is available
- [ ] Each `SessionInfo` in session/list response has `_meta.historyJsonlPath` when persistence is available
- [ ] `_meta` is `None` when no persistence store is configured (tests for this path)
- [ ] `cargo test` passes
- [ ] `cargo clippy` passes
- [ ] `cargo fmt` passes
- [ ] No new unwrap/expect/panic

## Testing

- Add tests in `acp/tests.rs` and `session/tests.rs` verifying `_meta` content
- Test with and without persistence store
- Verify the path is absolute and points to a valid history.jsonl location
close_reason: Implemented: NewSessionResponse, LoadSessionResponse, ResumeSessionResponse, and SessionInfo (in session/list) all include _meta.historyJsonlPath when persistence is configured. 5 new tests verify the field. All 313 tests pass, fmt/clippy clean.

title: M1: Read-only tool loop
description:
## Problem Statement

A chat-only agent cannot investigate a codebase. The model must read files and search before it can help.

## Solution

Add the agentic tool loop with read-only tools: read_file (via client fs when advertised, else local), list_dir/glob/grep (local), each reported through tool_call/tool_call_update updates. No permission prompts (read-only).

## User Stories

1. As a user, I want the agent to read files, so that its answers reflect my actual code.
2. As a user, I want the agent to search/glob/list the project, so that it locates relevant code on its own.
3. As a user, I want each tool call shown in my editor, so that I can see what the agent is doing.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Given DeepSeek returns tool_calls for read-only tools, when the loop runs, then each is reported as tool_call (pending) then tool_call_update (completed) and the result is fed back to the model.
2. Given read_file and client advertises fs.readTextFile, then the read goes through fs/read_text_file (respecting unsaved buffers); otherwise a local read.
3. Given grep/glob/list_dir, then they execute locally (ripgrep engine / globset) and return bounded results.
4. Given the model issues further tool calls, then the loop iterates until none remain, or the iteration cap (default ~25) trips returning StopReason::max_turn_requests.

## Implementation Notes

- Tool JSON-schema registry passed in the DeepSeek tools param; accumulate streamed tool-call arguments by index.
- Search via ignore + grep crates; glob via globset.
- Capability-driven routing of read_file (client vs local).

## Testing Notes

- Mock model emitting tool calls; assert the tool_call/tool_call_update sequence and history feedback. Test the max_turn_requests cap.

## Out of Scope

Mutating tools, permissions, shell.
close_reason: Read-only tool loop implemented and verified

title: Support ACP v1 MCP stdio servers
description:
ACP v1 Session Setup says clients may include mcpServers in session/new and all agents MUST support connecting to MCP servers via stdio. The adapter currently stores cwd/additionalDirectories but does not consume request.mcp_servers or connect MCP servers.

Implement support for stdio MCP servers supplied in session/new. The implementation should keep external dependencies out of domain logic, avoid leaking secrets in logs, and expose MCP tools to the model/tool loop as appropriate.

Evidence:
- deepseek-acp-adapter registers session/new only and handle_new_session_request ignores MCP server configs in src/main.rs.
- ACP v1 session setup requires stdio MCP support for agents.
- codex-acp initializes MCP-related capabilities and parses supplied MCP servers as a reference.

Acceptance criteria:
- session/new accepts stdio MCP server definitions and starts/connects them for the session.
- MCP tool results can participate in the prompt tool loop or an explicit unsupported path is returned per tool.
- Tests cover stdio MCP configuration parsing, launch failure, and successful tool exposure.

title: M4: Model selection, image input & docs polish
description:
## Problem Statement

v0.1 runs a fixed model, handles text+files only, and first-run setup is non-obvious (GUI editors may not inherit DEEPSEEK_API_KEY from the shell). Polish for real-world use.

## Solution

Wire the unstable session/set_model + availableModels (v4-flash / v4-pro / v4-pro+thinking) for capable clients with DEEPSEEK_MODEL as the default; map ACP image content blocks to DeepSeek image_url parts; write the README including the GUI env-inheritance gotcha.

## User Stories

1. As a Zed user, I want to switch models mid-session, so that I can trade cost/quality per task.
2. As a CodeCompanion user, I want a sensible default model from env, so that it works without a picker.
3. As a user, I want to send images, so that the model can use screenshots/diagrams.
4. As a new user, I want clear setup docs, so that DEEPSEEK_API_KEY is found even when launched from a GUI editor.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Given a client supporting set_model, then availableModels are advertised and session/set_model switches the active model; otherwise DEEPSEEK_MODEL (default deepseek-v4-pro) is used.
2. Given an ACP prompt with image content, then it is forwarded as an OpenAI-style image_url content part to DeepSeek.
3. Given the README, then it documents env-only config and the agent_servers.env workaround for GUI-launched editors.

## Implementation Notes

- set_model lives behind the crate's unstable feature; a graceful no-op for clients lacking it.

## Testing Notes

- set_model present vs absent; image content mapping.

## Out of Scope

Auto model-router, MCP client support, loadSession.

title: Populate title and updated_at on SessionInfo for session/list and session/resume
description:
## Motivation

When the user runs `/resume` inside Neovim (via deepseek-acp-adapter), they only see session IDs in the picker — no context about what the session was about or when it was last active. This makes it hard to choose which session to resume.

## What ACP Provides

The `SessionInfo` struct already has:

- **`title: Option<String>`** — intended as a human-readable label for the session (e.g., derived from the first user message)
- **`updated_at: Option<String>`** — ISO 8601 timestamp of last activity
- **`meta: Option<Meta>`** — a `serde_json::Map<String, Value>` for arbitrary extra data

The `session/resume` response also has `_meta` for agent-to-client extensibility.

## What Needs to Change

### 1. Adapter: populate `title` and `updated_at`

- `src/session.rs` `SessionStore::list_sessions()` — add `.title(...)` and `.updated_at(...)` when constructing `SessionInfo`
- `src/session_store.rs` `FilesystemSessionStore::list_persisted()` — same

### 2. Store the needed data

- `title`: derive from the first user `ChatMessage` in the session history (truncated to ~80 chars)
- `updated_at`: store a last-activity timestamp on `SessionRecord` and persist it in `PersistedSessionMeta`

### 3. Persist the new fields

- Add `title` and `updated_at` to `PersistedSessionMeta`
- Handle backward compat when loading old meta files (no title -> show session_id)

### 4. (Optional) Emit `session_info_update` notifications

The protocol has a `session_info_update` notification that can push metadata changes to the client during a session. Currently listed as not emitted.

## Client-Side

The Neovim CodeCompanion plugin would also need to display these fields in its session picker — that's out of scope for this crate, but populating the data unlocks it.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] New sessions get a `title` derived from the first user prompt
- [ ] `updated_at` is set on session creation and bumped on each `session/prompt`
- [ ] Old persisted sessions (no title/updated_at) don't break
- [ ] `session/list` returns `title` and `updated_at` for all sessions
- [ ] No new unwrap/expect/panic
close_reason: Implemented: SessionInfo now returns title (derived from first user msg) and updated_at (ISO 8601 timestamp) for session/list and session/resume. Backward compat with old persisted sessions.

title: Handle non-UTF-8 file reads without internal ACP error
description:
When using `deepseek_acp_adapter`, a read-file tool call against a Cargo target fingerprint artifact fails as an internal ACP/JSON-RPC error instead of returning a controlled tool error.

Observed log excerpt:

```text
2026-06-04T06:29:21.735774Z DEBUG connection{name="deepseek-acp-adapter"}: agent_client_protocol::jsonrpc::outgoing_actor: send_raw_message Notification { untyped: UntypedMessage { method: "session/update", params: Object {"sessionId": String("session-87b8ba86-3228-48c5-8660-b15e04c14b9f"), "update": Object {"kind": String("read"), "rawInput": Object {"path": String("target/debug/.fingerprint/agent-client-protocol-077e92246336e4d0/dep-lib-agent_client_protocol")}, "sessionUpdate": String("tool_call"), "title": String("read_file"), "toolCallId": String("call_00_LH4DZ1YxP8fpuPD71CBj3477")}} } } tx=UnboundedSender { closed: false }
2026-06-04T06:29:21.735831Z DEBUG connection{name="deepseek-acp-adapter"}: agent_client_protocol::jsonrpc::outgoing_actor: outgoing_protocol_actor Notification { untyped: UntypedMessage { method: "session/update", params: Object {"sessionId": String("session-87b8ba86-3228-48c5-8660-b15e04c14b9f"), "update": Object {"kind": String("read"), "rawInput": Object {"path": String("target/debug/.fingerprint/agent-client-protocol-077e92246336e4d0/dep-lib-agent_client_protocol")}, "sessionUpdate": String("tool_call"), "title": String("read_file"), "toolCallId": String("call_00_LH4DZ1YxP8fpuPD71CBj3477")}} } }
2026-06-04T06:29:21.735868Z DEBUG connection{name="deepseek-acp-adapter"}: agent_client_protocol::jsonrpc::outgoing_actor: outgoing_protocol_actor Request { id: String("51b88be8-e5ea-46b9-ab8f-dd922ac39597"), method: "fs/read_text_file", role_id: Singleton("agent_client_protocol::role::acp::Client", TypeId(0x5550ea44545b2b99be47548edd5c74c4)), untyped: UntypedMessage { method: "fs/read_text_file", params: Object {"limit": Number(200), "line": Number(1), "path": String("/home/lotso/code/deepseek-acp-adapter/target/debug/.fingerprint/agent-client-protocol-077e92246336e4d0/dep-lib-agent_client_protocol"), "sessionId": String("session-87b8ba86-3228-48c5-8660-b15e04c14b9f")} }, response_tx: Sender { complete: false } }
error: Internal error: {
  "data": "stream did not contain valid UTF-8",
```
